does: This paper implements role-based access control (RBAC) mechanisms for AI agents in industrial settings to mitigate security vulnerabilities. The work addresses the specific threat of prompt injection attacks by designing and deploying access control policies that restrict what actions agents can perform based on assigned roles. The approach leverages LLMs as the core component of autonomous agents that interact with external tools and real-time data sources, and the authors demonstrate how RBAC can limit the surface area for attacks while maintaining agent functionality for legitimate industrial applications like predictive maintenance, process optimization, and real-time decision-making in manufacturing environments.
connects: This work builds on the broader literature of autonomous agents powered by LLMs, which have gained prominence as systems that overcome static training data limitations through dynamic tool access and real-time information retrieval. The paper directly addresses emerging security challenges in agent-based systems, particularly prompt injection attacks that have been identified as critical vulnerabilities in LLM applications. It connects to classical access control frameworks in cybersecurity and systems design, adapting established RBAC principles from traditional software security to the novel domain of LLM-powered agents. The motivation also engages with recent work on agent safety and the operational risks of deploying LLMs in high-stakes industrial contexts where failures can have significant consequences.
enables: This work establishes foundational security practices for deploying AI agents in real industrial environments where access control is operationally critical. By demonstrating RBAC implementation for LLM agents, it opens pathways for standardizing security policies across industrial AI deployments and creates frameworks that other organizations can adapt. The approach potentially enables broader industrial adoption of autonomous agent systems by reducing security concerns and providing auditable, role-based restrictions on agent capabilities. Future work could extend these mechanisms to other threat vectors beyond prompt injection, integrate RBAC with formal verification methods for agent behavior, and develop dynamic access control policies that adapt to evolving threat landscapes in industrial settings.
